ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for cucumber raita; A00GG (yoghurt-based sauce) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cucumber raita as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cucumber raita is approximately 100 g (about 4–5 tablespoons), served as a cooling side dish alongside biryani, paratha, or other spiced Indian meals.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Cucumber raita is a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using component-level data for plain low-fat yogurt and raw cucumber, combined in a typical 80:20 yogurt-to-cucumber ratio. Values for minor micronutrients (phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, B-vitamins) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284) and raw cucumber with peel (FDC ID 168409). Vitamin B12 value of 0.4 mcg per 100 g is consistent with the yogurt component contribution per ICMR-NIN and USDA data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cucumber raita as a composite dish. GI estimated at 20 based on the dominant yogurt component (plain low-fat yogurt GI ≈ 14–36, mean ~20 per Atkinson et al. 2008) and the negligible carbohydrate contribution of raw cucumber (GI ~15). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (20 × 7.2) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content with beneficial potassium and calcium
hypertension.guidance: Cucumber raita is naturally low in sodium (~40 mg per 100 g) and provides potassium (170 mg) and calcium (110 mg), which support blood pressure regulation; however, individuals should limit added black salt (kala namak) or table salt during preparation to keep sodium intake controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~20) and moderate protein from yogurt
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 20 and 3.5 g protein per 100 g from the yogurt base, cucumber raita produces a minimal postprandial glucose response and can be consumed freely as a side dish; individuals should use plain, unsweetened yogurt and avoid adding sugar or sweetened variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and probiotic yogurt base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cucumber raita contains only ~1.5 g total fat and ~0.9 g saturated fat per 100 g with no trans fat, and the probiotic cultures in yogurt may modestly support lipid profiles; it is a heart-friendly accompaniment when prepared with low-fat plain yogurt and minimal added salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic content of yogurt may modulate gut-mediated inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The live cultures in plain yogurt may provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit for some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those with dairy sensitivity should monitor personal response and consider lactose-free yogurt alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cucumber Raita (खीरा रायता, Kheera Raita) is a classic Indian yogurt-based accompaniment prepared by combining grated or finely chopped raw cucumber with whisked plain yogurt (dahi), seasoned with roasted cumin powder (jeera), black salt (kala namak), and optionally garnished with fresh coriander or mint leaves. The dish requires no cooking and is assembled by mixing the ingredients just before serving. The texture is smooth and creamy from the yogurt with a refreshing crunch from the cucumber, and the flavour is mildly tangy, cooling, and lightly spiced. Cucumber raita is a staple accompaniment across the Indian subcontinent, served alongside biryani, pulao, paratha, and other spiced dishes to balance heat and aid digestion. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 60 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 1.5 g fat, and 7.2 g carbohydrate, with a low glycemic index of approximately 20. It is a good source of calcium (~110 mg), potassium (~170 mg), and vitamin B12 (~0.4 mcg) from the yogurt component. The dish is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its low GI, low sodium, and low saturated fat profile. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. The primary allergen is milk.
common_user_queries: Can I eat raita with biryani?
Is raita healthy for weight loss?
What are the ingredients in cucumber raita?
Is cucumber raita suitable for hypertension?
meal_context: Digestive aid alongside heavy Indian meals

confidence_reason: Cucumber raita is a composite dish not listed as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB databases. Nutrient values are derived by component-level adaptation using ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for plain yogurt and raw cucumber in a standard preparation ratio. GI is an analogous estimate based on the yogurt component from a peer-reviewed source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values reflect a standard cucumber raita preparation with approximately 80% plain low-fat yogurt and 20% raw cucumber by weight, seasoned with cumin and black salt. Actual values will vary with yogurt fat content and cucumber-to-yogurt ratio. 2. The input record described this food as 'Cucumber' with food_group 'Vegetables' and per_100g values consistent with raw cucumber only (energy 15 kcal, protein 0.7 g), which is inconsistent with the description of Cucumber Raita (a yogurt-based dish). Nutrient values have been corrected to reflect the composite raita preparation. 3. The input portions array contained duplicate and irrelevant entries ('6 pieces of maki') which have been removed. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for plain yogurt and raw cucumber per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; naturally zero in this preparation. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284). 6. The input listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as cucumber raita contains approximately 7.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is not consistent with strict ketogenic dietary standards. 7. Vitamin B12 of 0.4 mcg per 100 g is plausible for a yogurt-dominant preparation and is retained from the input, consistent with USDA data for plain yogurt. 8. No -1 sentinel values require special documentation beyond standard unavailability; all -1 fields in the template have been replaced with estimated or confirmed numeric values from approved sources. 9. GI source: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. doi:10.2337/dc08-1239.
